name: Suwa
description: Suwa was a pre-Islamic Arabian deity, specifically worshipped by the tribe of Hudhayl. He was a male deity, often associated with rain, water, and fertility. His origins are somewhat obscure, predating the rise of Islam, and his worship involved ritualistic practices aimed at ensuring agricultural abundance and the continuation of the tribe. While specific myths surrounding Suwa are fragmented and scarce in surviving historical records, he is understood to have been a significant figure in the religious landscape of pre-Islamic Arabia. He was viewed as a powerful force capable of influencing the natural world, particularly in the arid regions where water was a precious commodity. The Hudhayl tribe held Suwa in high regard, likely offering sacrifices and prayers to appease him and secure his blessings. After the advent of Islam, the idols of Suwa, like those of other pre-Islamic deities, were destroyed, and his worship ceased. However, his memory persists as a testament to the diverse and complex religious beliefs that once flourished in the Arabian Peninsula. The name Suwa is thought to have been derived from a root relating to the act of drinking or irrigation, further solidifying his connection to water and sustenance. Historical accounts depict him as a large stone idol, suggesting a tangible representation of divine power for his worshippers. The destruction of the idol symbolized the triumph of Islam and the rejection of the polytheistic practices of the past. The worship of Suwa reflects the importance of agriculture and water resources in the lives of the pre-Islamic Arabians. The stories of Suwa are intertwined with the history of the Hudhayl tribe, who saw him as a protector and provider.
subtitle: God of Rain and Water for Hudhayl

keyMyths.title: Suwa and the Drought
keyMyths.description: The Hudhayl tribe faced a severe drought. They prayed to Suwa, offering sacrifices, and eventually, rain came, saving their crops and livestock.
keyMyths.source: Accounts of pre-Islamic Arabian religious practices

keyMyths.title: The Idol of Suwa
keyMyths.description: The idol of Suwa was a large stone, revered by the Hudhayl. They would gather around it to perform rituals and seek blessings for their tribe.

keyMyths.title: Destruction of Suwa's Idol
keyMyths.description: After the rise of Islam, the idol of Suwa was destroyed as part of the campaign to eradicate pre-Islamic polytheism and establish the worship of Allah.
keyMyths.source: Islamic historical texts detailing the destruction of idols

sources.author: Al-Kalbi
sources.description: A key source on pre-Islamic Arabian religious practices, including descriptions of deities like Suwa.
sources.text: A key source on pre-Islamic Arabian religious practices, including descriptions of deities like Suwa.

sources.author: Ibn Ishaq
sources.work: Sirat Rasul Allah (The Life of Muhammad)
sources.description: Provides context for the religious landscape of Arabia at the time of Muhammad and the destruction of idols.
sources.text: Provides context for the religious landscape of Arabia at the time of Muhammad and the destruction of idols.
